aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c0_.source_text: [1] In the previous two lectures, we discussed outranking-based methods from the families of PROMETHEE and ELECTRE. You already know how to derive a choice, ranking, or sorting recommendation by comparing objects pairwise. In particular, we elaborated on the PROMETHEE II method, which orders the alternatives from the best to the worst by assigning a single number, called net flow, to each option. Today, we will build on a similar idea. We will address ranking problems by computing comprehensive measures of desirability for all alternatives. However, the way of computing these scores will be completely di ff erent than in the outranking-based methods. I will introduce you to a value-based model, which translates a performance on each criterion into a marginal value and then aggregates such partial values into a comprehensive measure. We will discuss a broad family of methods, starting with traditional approaches where the preference information needs to be provided directly. However, our main hero will be a preference disaggregation method, called UTA. It accepts indirect preference in the form of holistic judgments for a subset of alternatives. Such information is used to construct a preference model that is subsequently employed to evaluate all alternatives. Even if we start with ranking problems, we will end up with sorting to show how the preference disaggregation concept can be adapted to various problem types.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c1_.answer: Ordering alternatives from best to worst.
aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c1_.source_text: [2] Let us start with multiple criteria ranking. We aim at ordering the alternatives from the best to the worst by using a relative comparison approach. We focus on deriving a complete ranking, which admits only preference and indi ff erence relations. Clearly, preference for one alternative implies a higher position in the ranking, whereas indi ff erence is equivalent to sharing the same rank. Moreover, we pay attention to methods deriving a score for each alternative. In this way, the ranking can be perceived as cardinal as opposed to the ordinal rankings. The latter ones may be constructed step by step in the up-down or bottom-top fashion without evaluating alternatives on a cardinal scale. Please note that when dealing with ranking, you also, somehow, by the way, address a choice problem. Simply, the most preferred alternative can be identified as the one that is ranked at the top.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c2_.answer: Rank candidates from best to worst based on performance on preference-ordered criteria.
aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c2_.source_text: [3] Our running example refers to medium size firm that produces tools for agriculture. Its Chief Executive O ffi cer intends to double the production in the next two years. For this, he needs to hire a new sales manager. A recruitment agency has interviewed candidates for the position and evaluated them in terms of three relevant criteria. Sales management experience is a gain-type attribute with a scale from 0 to 10. Human qualities are quantified similarly, whereas the expected salary is to be minimized, and we note an exact number in thousands of euros. At this stage, we have the alternatives and their performances on preference-ordered criteria. The aim is to rank the candidates from the best to the worst. For the interpretability of results, we wish to assign a score to each candidate.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c3_.answer: Assignment of numerals to objects or events according to pre-defined rules.
aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c3_.source_text: [4] The methods we will discuss derive from the concepts of measurement and evaluation. Measurement is defined as the assignment of numerals to objects or events according to some pre-defined rules, whereas evaluation is the measurement of values. We will focus on measuring the values of Decision Makers. For this, we will use a tool called a marginal value function. Its role for a given criterion is to capture the preference of each alternative and the di ff erences of preferences. In the slide, you have an example marginal value function expressing the value of salary. Using it, you can learn that the value of 12 thousand euro is 0.25, and it is much lesser than the value of 10 thousand euro. Also, it is possible to note that the di ff erence of preference between 10 and 12 thousand euro is vastly more significant than the di ff erence between 12 and 15 thousand euro. This means that the Decision Maker highly values the low salaries of potential candidates, whereas, in his eyes, the di ff erences between high salaries are not so great because, in general, they are low appreciated. We can consider such marginal functions for other criteria. The one in the left-down corner shows how to quantify the value of experience. Assuming that the criteria are separable, we can now compare the preference di ff erences between various viewpoints.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c4_.source_text: [5] The marginal value functions quantify the preferences on each criterion, using a kind of interval scale between 0 and 1. Thanks to this, you can compare both di ff erences and ratios of preference degrees. However, to evaluate the alternatives, we need to make a step from the per-criterion level to a comprehensive layer involving all criteria at the same. For this, we need to aggregate the marginal values into a comprehensive one. At this stage, we implement an intuitive rule saying that more important criteria should have a greater impact on the comprehensive value. All in all, such a value is defined as a weighted sum of marginal values over all criteria. The model collects contributions from all criteria and sums them up while potentially di ff erentiating their impacts. Please note that it makes sense only when the preferences on each criterion are independent of the remaining criteria. Such a value-based model is fully compensatory. This means that good values on one criterion can compensate for bad values on other criteria. Also, when you assign a single score to each alternative, only preference and indi ff erence relations can be instantiated. A preference holds if one alternative has a higher comprehensive value than another, whereas indi ff erence requires the same values.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c5_.answer: Breaking down complex information into smaller parts.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c6_.answer: Historical cost, replacement cost, and market value are common methods.
aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c6_.source_text: What is the empirical evidence for value measurement?

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c7_.answer: Subjective preferences from direct or indirect observation and relatively easy computation.
aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c7_.source_text: - revealed preferences from customersÕ behaviour in markets - subjective preferences from direct or indirect observation and relatively easy computation. [6] Let us accustom with an additive value function. In the upper part of the slide, we present the marginal value functions for the three criteria in our illustrative study. Their weights suggest that the first criterion has the greatest impact on the comprehensive value, and the second criterion has the least influence. To order the alternatives from the best to the worst, we need to read o ff the marginal value on each criterion. Once the performances of alternatives correspond to the breakpoints of marginal value functions, it is easy. Otherwise, we need to use linear interpolation. For example, on the first criterion 9 is between 7 that is assigned a value of 0.5, and 10 assigned a value of 1. Since it is in the two-thirds of the sub-range, we assign it a value of 0.833. In the second stage, the marginal values are aggregated into the comprehensive ones, using a weighted sum model. You can see an example of such aggregation at the bottom of the slide for Andreev, alternative A. Finally, the candidates are ordered in the decreasing order of their comprehensive values. In our case, we would select Ferret, alternative F , with the highest comprehensive value, and we would surely reject Calvet, alternative C, which is ranked at the bottom.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c8_.answer: Requires specification of extreme performances for values 0 and 1, and careful indication of maximization or minimization.
aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c8_.source_text: [7] We know how to use an additive value model, but we do not know how it can be specified. We will start with traditional methods requiring direct preference information. There are many di ff erent approaches that can be used for eliciting marginal value functions, but one of the most widely used is the bisection method. It requires the specification of the extreme performances that correspond to values 0 and 1. Here, you need to be already very careful to indicate whether a criterion is to be maximized or minimized. Later, you should indicate a mid-point such that the change of the value from 0-value performance to it and from it to 1-value performance is the same. This process may be continued to bisect the resulting value intervals to better emphasize the convexity or concavity of the function's shape. In the middle part of the slide, we present the process of defining such a function for the second criterion, whereas in the bottom part, we depict the marginal value functions for all three criteria. As you can see, they have various shapes. For example, the functions for g1 and g3 are concave, whereas the function for g2 is convex. It represents a riskaverse attitude, where relatively high values are assigned even to rather bad performances, and the di ff erences between the most preferred performances are not that great.

aea6567a-15a3-4c98-8ebc-5ded95303d6b_c9_.answer: Represents the weight of each group in the chunk's overall ranking.
